title: On Gromov’s Waist of the Sphere Theorem
overall_summary: The paper gives a detailed proof of Gromov's waist theorem for the unit sphere: for every continuous map f:S^n→R^k, a fiber has ε-neighborhood at least as large as the ε-neighborhood of an equatorial S^{n-k}. The proof combines a generalized Borsuk-Ulam theorem for convex partitions of the sphere, a refinement into low-dimensional pancakes, compactness of convexely derived measures, one-dimensional sin-concavity estimates, and disintegration over limiting infinite convex partitions. The most transferable material for the rectangle dilation problem is the machinery for forcing a common fiber to meet many convex pieces, then converting local convex-measure lower bounds into global volume lower bounds. The pancake construction is especially relevant because the paper explicitly identifies Euclidean rectangles as model pancakes: an n-rectangle is close to k-dimensional precisely when n-k side lengths are small.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (Gromov waist of the sphere)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S^n be the unit n-sphere with its canonical Riemannian volume, let q≤n, and let f:S^n→R^q be continuous. Let S^{n-q} denote an equatorial subsphere of S^n. The theorem states that there exists z∈R^q such that, for every ε>0, vol_n(f^{-1}(z)+ε) ≥ vol_n(S^{n-q}+ε), where A+ε denotes the ε-tubular neighborhood of A in S^n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is built in several layers. First, a Gromov-Borsuk-Ulam partition theorem produces finite equal-volume partitions of S^n into convex sets whose chosen centers have the same f-image. Second, by restricting allowed cutting hyperplanes and passing to large numbers of cuts, these partitions can be made into q-pancakes, so in the limit one obtains convex partitions supported on convexely derived measures of dimension at most q. Third, convexely derived measures supported on d-dimensional convex sets have continuous densities that are sin^{n-d}-concave along geodesics; a one-dimensional comparison with cos^{n-d}(t) gives the sharp lower bound μ(B(M_0(μ),ε)) ≥ vol(S^{n-d}+ε)/vol(S^n), where M_0(μ) is the unique density maximum. Fourth, the paper defines r-centers M_r(μ) as maximizers of x↦μ(B(x,r)) and proves that M_r(μ) converges to M_0(μ) as r→0, uniformly on compact families of fixed-dimensional measures. Fifth, for a smooth generic f and partitions Π_r adapted to f, low-dimensional pieces are shown to have asymptotically zero weight by a weak concavity estimate for ball masses and the fact that max_z vol(f^{-1}(z)+r)=O(r^q). Hence almost all limiting weight lies on q-dimensional pieces, and integrating the local lower bound over the convex partition gives max_z vol(f^{-1}(z)+ε) ≥ vol(S^{n-q}+ε). Finally, continuous f are obtained by uniform approximation by smooth generic maps and a Hausdorff/Fatou semicontinuity argument for tubular neighborhoods.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (Gromov-Borsuk-Ulam theorem for convex partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a continuous center map c from the space of open geodesically convex subsets of S^n to S^n. Let f:S^n→R^q be continuous with q≤n. For every integer i≥1, there exists a partition of S^n into 2^i open convex sets S_1,...,S_{2^i}, all of equal n-dimensional spherical volume, such that f(c(S_1))=...=f(c(S_{2^i})).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof parameterizes special convex partitions by a product P_i=(S^n)^{2^i-1}, whose factors are indexed by internal vertices of the rooted binary tree T_i of depth i. A point of P_i assigns an oriented great hypersphere to each internal vertex; following a path from the root to a leaf and choosing the positive or negative side at each stage gives one convex piece. The automorphism group Aut(T_i), generated by switching descendants at vertices, acts freely on P_i and identifies oriented descriptions of the same unoriented partition. For a partition point p∈P_i and for each terminal cutting hyperplane h, let S_h be the convex piece on one chosen side, v(h)=vol(S_h), and φ(h)=v(h) f(c(S_h)). Extend v and φ upward by summing over descendant leaves. Define an equivariant map F:P_i→(R^{q+1})^{2^i-1} by assigning to each internal node h the pair (v(h)-v(-h), φ(h)-φ(-h)). Equivariance lets F descend to a section of the vector bundle L_i=(P_i×(R^{q+1})^{2^i-1})/Aut(T_i) over Q_i=P_i/Aut(T_i). A Stiefel-Whitney computation shows that the top Stiefel-Whitney class of L_i is nonzero, so every section has a zero. At a zero of F, each binary split has equal volume and equal weighted f-center sums. Inducting down the binary tree gives equal volume for all leaves and equality of all f(c(S_j)).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 2.2-2.3 (topology of the binary partition bundle)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let T_i be the rooted binary tree used to encode i-step partitions of S^n. Lemma 2.2 identifies the automorphism group recursively as Aut(T_i)=Aut(T_{i-1}) wr Z_2, an iterated wreath product, with the Z_2 factors switching the two children of vertices at the previous level. Lemma 2.3 states that for the vector bundle L_i=(P_i×(R^{q+1})^{2^i-1})/Aut(T_i) over Q_i=P_i/Aut(T_i), where P_i=(S^n)^{2^i-1} and the action is the natural one on tree-indexed factors, the top Stiefel-Whitney class w_top(L_i) is nonzero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 2.2, Aut(T_{i-1}) acts by permuting vertices above the last level, while an independent Z_2 switch is available at each vertex just above the leaves. These switches form a normal subgroup (Z_2)^I, where I is the set of level i-1 vertices, and conjugation by Aut(T_{i-1}) permutes the factors, giving the wreath-product semidirect product. For Lemma 2.3, use the splitting P_i=P_{i-1}×(S^n)^{2^{i-1}} and the induced fibration Q_i→Q_{i-1} with fiber (RP^n)^{2^{i-1}}. On each fiber, the new part of L_i restricts to a sum of copies of the tautological line bundle γ_n over RP^n, repeated q+1 times for each new factor. Thus L_i decomposes as p_i^*L_{i-1} plus these tautological summands. Since w(γ_n)=1+a and powers a^j are nonzero in H^*(RP^n;Z_2) up to degree n, the top Stiefel-Whitney class multiplies inductively by a nonzero fiber class. Starting from the i=1 case gives w_top(L_i)≠0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4 (pancake partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let f:S^n→R^q be continuous, with q≤n, and keep a fixed continuous center map on open convex subsets. A convex set C⊂S^n is called a (q,ε)-pancake if every point of C is within distance ε of some q-dimensional convex subset of S^n. For every ε>0 there exists i_0 such that for all i≥i_0 there is a partition of S^n into 2^i open convex subsets such that each piece is a (q,ε)-pancake, all pieces have equal volume, and the centers of all pieces have the same image under f.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof modifies the partition construction of Theorem 3 by restricting the available cutting hyperplanes. If L is an (n-q-1)-dimensional linear subspace through the origin in R^{n+1}, then oriented hyperplanes containing L are parametrized by the unit sphere in L^⊥, identified with S^{q+1}. Since the target has dimension q, the same Borsuk-Ulam/Stiefel-Whitney argument applies when the cutting normal at a stage is required to lie in such an S^{q+1}. A compactness lemma on the Grassmannian supplies finitely many planes L_1,...,L_N whose associated restricted direction spheres form an ε-net in the sense needed to hit balls of cutting directions. Repeating enough stages with these restrictions forces every final convex piece to contain no ball of definite radius in any (q+1)-sphere section. A Blaschke-selection compactness argument then implies that any sequence of convex sets with this no-(q+1)-ball property converges to a convex set of dimension at most q; hence, for sufficiently many cuts, every piece lies within ε of a q-dimensional convex set. The equal-volume and common-image properties are inherited from Theorem 3.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.1 (density and sin-concavity of convexely derived measures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S⊂S^n be a geodesically convex set of dimension d≤n. Let μ be a convexely derived probability measure supported on S, meaning that μ is a vague limit of normalized restrictions vol|S_j/vol(S_j) for open convex sets S_j⊂S^n. Then μ has a continuous density f with respect to the canonical d-dimensional Riemannian volume on S. Moreover, along every geodesic arc contained in S, the density f is sin^{n-d}-concave: equivalently, f^{1/(n-d)}, transported to an arc of the unit circle, extends to a 1-homogeneous concave function on the corresponding planar cone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take the Euclidean cones co(S_j)⊂R^{n+1}; they converge to co(S). The normalized Euclidean measures on co(S_j) converge to a convexely derived measure μ' on co(S). A Euclidean convex-geometry result cited from Gromov-Milman gives μ'=F dm_{d+1}, where F is (n-d)-concave. Because the cone construction comes from spherical volume and radial scaling, μ' is (n+1)-homogeneous while (d+1)-dimensional Lebesgue measure is (d+1)-homogeneous, so F is (n-d)-homogeneous. Restricting F to the cone over any geodesic arc σ⊂S gives an (n-d)-concave and (n-d)-homogeneous function on a two-dimensional cone. Therefore F^{1/(n-d)} is concave and 1-homogeneous there, which is exactly sin-concavity after restriction to the unit circle. The restriction to S gives the asserted spherical density f.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.3-4.7 (one-dimensional sin-concavity comparison)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let m>0. A nonnegative sin^m-concave function on an interval has at most one maximum point and no local minimum. If f is a nonzero nonnegative sin^m-concave function on [0,τ] whose maximum is attained at 0, then τ≤π/2. More precisely, if 0<ε<π/2 and h(t)=c cos^m(t) is chosen so that h(ε)=f(ε), then f(t)≥h(t) for 0≤t≤ε and f(t)≤h(t) for ε≤t≤τ. Consequently, for every α≥0 and ε≤π/2, one has the integral comparison ∫_0^{min(ε,τ)} f(t) sin^α(t) dt / ∫_0^τ f(t) sin^α(t) dt ≥ ∫_0^ε cos^m(t) sin^α(t) dt / ∫_0^{π/2} cos^m(t) sin^α(t) dt.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The uniqueness of the maximum and absence of local minima follow by writing g=f^{1/m} as the restriction of a 1-homogeneous concave function G on a planar cone. If two circle points had equal maxima, concavity and homogeneity would force a larger value at the normalized midpoint; a local minimum is excluded similarly by representing it as a normalized midpoint. For the comparison with cos^m, reduce to m=1 by taking m-th roots and symmetrize f by g(t)=f(|t|). A gluing lemma shows that this symmetrized function remains sin-concave because the one-sided derivatives at the maximum have the correct order. Let G be the homogeneous concave extension of g and let H(x,y)=cx, the homogeneous extension of c cos(t). Since G-H is concave and vanishes at the two symmetric points with angular distance ε, its sign is nonnegative in the sector between them and nonpositive outside. This yields the pointwise comparison. Multiplying by sin^α(t) and integrating on [0,ε] and [ε,τ] gives the displayed ratio inequality; the same comparison also rules out τ>π/2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.8-4.9 (sharp lower bound for balls in convexely derived measures and equatorial tube formula)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a convexely derived probability measure supported on a d-dimensional convex set S⊂S^n with d<n. Let M_0(μ) be the unique point where the density of μ with respect to d-dimensional volume attains its maximum. Then for every ε>0, μ(B(M_0(μ),ε)) ≥ [∫_0^ε cos^{n-d}(t) sin^{d-1}(t) dt] / [∫_0^{π/2} cos^{n-d}(t) sin^{d-1}(t) dt]. In addition, if S^{n-d} is an equatorial subsphere of S^n, then vol_n(S^{n-d}+ε)/vol_n(S^n) equals the same quotient of integrals.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use polar coordinates (t,u) centered at M_0(μ) inside the d-sphere containing S. Convexity of S gives a radial boundary function τ(u) so that points of S in direction u have 0≤t≤τ(u). Write dμ=f(t,u) sin^{d-1}(t) dt du. For each fixed direction u, the function t↦f(t,u) is sin^{n-d}-concave and has its maximum at t=0, so Lemma 4.7 with α=d-1 gives a lower bound for the fraction of the ray-mass lying in 0≤t≤ε by the model cos^{n-d} fraction. Integrating over u gives the stated lower bound for μ(B(M_0(μ),ε)). For the equatorial tube formula, push forward spherical volume under the distance-to-S^{n-d} map; the density is a constant times cos^{n-d}(t) sin^{d-1}(t) dt on [0,π/2], and normalization cancels the constant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 and Corollary 5.3 (Bishop-Gromov lower bound for convex pieces and their limits)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every open convex set S⊂S^n, every x∈S, and every r, vol(S∩B(x,r))/vol(S) ≥ vol(B(x,r))/vol(S^n). If μ is any convexely derived probability measure on S^n and x belongs to support(μ), then μ(B(x,r)) ≥ vol(B(x,r))/vol(S^n).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For an open convex S, apply Bishop-Gromov comparison on the unit sphere: the ratio vol(S∩B(x,r))/vol(B(x,r)) is nonincreasing in r. Comparing its value at radius r to its limiting value when the ball fills S^n gives the displayed inequality. For a convexely derived limit μ=lim vol|S_j/vol(S_j), first extract a subsequence whose supports Hausdorff-converge to a compact convex set containing support(μ). If x∈support(μ), choose x_j∈S_j with x_j→x. Since metric ball boundaries have zero μ-mass in the relevant cases, vague convergence of measures on sets with null boundary passes the finite inequality to the limit.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.4-5.6 (compactness and continuity for convexely derived measures)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let MC be the vague closure of normalized volume measures of open convex subsets of S^n. Let MC_0 be the measures with zero-dimensional support and MC_+=MC\MC_0. The support map μ↦support(μ), from MC to compact subsets of S^n with the Hausdorff metric, is continuous. If MC_ρ denotes measures in MC whose support has diameter at least ρ>0, then sup_{μ∈MC_ρ, x∈S^n} μ(B(x,r)) tends to 0 as r→0. Finally, the function (μ,x,r)↦μ(B(x,r)) is continuous on MC_+×S^n×[0,π/2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For support continuity, if μ_j→μ and supports S_j Hausdorff-converge to S, test functions supported away from S show support(μ)⊂S. Conversely, if x∈S, take x_j∈S_j→x and use the Bishop-Gromov lower bound from Corollary 5.3 on a bump function supported near x to show that μ gives positive mass to every neighborhood of x. For uniform small-ball mass on MC_ρ, reduce locally to Euclidean space. If B=B(x,r) and support(μ) has a point y at distance at least ρ/2 from x, translate so y is the origin in the affine span. Concavity and homogeneity of the density imply μ(λB)≥λ^n μ(B) for λ∈(0,1). Choosing N≈ρ/(4r) disjoint dilates λB with λ between 1/2 and 1 gives μ(B)≤C(n) r/ρ. For continuity of μ(B(x,r)), move x_j to x by isometries tending to the identity, use vague convergence and the fact that non-Dirac convexely derived measures give zero mass to ball boundaries, and then use Dini's lemma to make the dependence on r continuous.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.8 (disintegration formula for infinite convex partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let CP be the vague closure, in the space of probability measures on MC, of finite convex partitions Π of S^n encoded by m(Π)=Σ_S [vol(S)/vol(S^n)] δ_{μ_S}, where μ_S=vol|S/vol(S) and the sum runs over the pieces S. Let A⊂S^n be a set such that ∂A has zero ℓ-dimensional volume in every ℓ-dimensional subsphere, for every 0<ℓ<n. If Π∈CP and Π(MC_0)=0, then vol(A)/vol(S^n)=∫_{MC} μ(A) dΠ(μ).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a finite convex partition, the identity is simply additivity of volume over the pieces after normalizing each piece. The boundary hypothesis ensures, via the vague convergence lemma for Radon measures on sets whose boundary has zero measure, that μ↦μ(A) is continuous on MC_+. Therefore the finite-partition identity passes to vague limits m(Π_j)→Π, provided Π gives no mass to MC_0. This yields the stated disintegration over the limiting convex partition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.9 and Corollary 5.10 (semicontinuity of r-centers and closedness of adapted partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For μ∈MC and r>0, define v_{r,μ}(x)=μ(B(x,r)) and let M_r(μ) be the set of points in support(μ) where v_{r,μ} is maximal. If μ_i→μ∈MC_+, then every subsequential limit of points x_i∈M_r(μ_i) lies in M_r(μ), and every subsequential limit of points in the spherical convex hulls conv(M_r(μ_i)) lies in conv(M_r(μ)). For a continuous map f:S^n→R^q, define F_r to be the set of convex partitions Π∈CP for which ⋂_{μ∈support(Π)} f(conv(M_r(μ))) is nonempty. Then F_r is closed in CP.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first assertion, let x_i∈M_r(μ_i) converge to x. For any y∈support(μ), support continuity gives y_i∈support(μ_i) with y_i→y. Continuity of ball masses gives v_{r,μ}(x)=lim v_{r,μ_i}(x_i) and v_{r,μ}(y)=lim v_{r,μ_i}(y_i). Since each x_i maximizes v_{r,μ_i}, x maximizes v_{r,μ}. For convex hulls, pass to cones and use Carathéodory: any limit of convex combinations of points from M_r(μ_i) is a convex combination of limit points from M_r(μ). For closedness of F_r, take Π_i→Π with Π_i∈F_r and choose z_i in the common intersection. After passing to a convergent subsequence z_i→z, every μ∈support(Π) is a limit of μ_i∈support(Π_i); the convex-hull semicontinuity and continuity of f imply z∈f(conv(M_r(μ))) for all μ in support(Π).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 5.11 (existence of adapted low-dimensional convex partitions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every r>0, the set F_r of r-adapted convex partitions contains uniform atomic measures coming from finite convex partitions with arbitrarily many pieces. Moreover, using the pancake partitions of Theorem 4 and closedness of F_r, one obtains elements of F_r∩CP_{≤q}, where CP_{≤q} denotes convex partitions supported on convexely derived measures whose supports have dimension at most q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The finite adapted partitions come from applying Theorem 3 with a center construction tied to M_r. Theorem 4 allows these finite partitions to be chosen with pieces lying in arbitrarily thin neighborhoods of q-dimensional convex sets. Passing to the compact space CP and using the closedness of F_r from Corollary 5.10 gives a limiting convex partition that is still r-adapted to f and whose support lies in MC_{≤q}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.12-5.16 (r-centers converge to density maxima)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let μ be a convexely derived measure supported on a d-dimensional convex set with d<n, and let M_0(μ) be the unique point where its density is maximal. Then d_H(M_r(μ),M_0(μ))→0 as r→0. More strongly, for each fixed d<n, if μ'→μ within MC_d and r→0, then d_H(M_r(μ'),M_0(μ))→0; consequently, on every compact subset of MC_d, the functions μ↦d_H(M_r(μ),M_0(μ)) converge uniformly to 0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a fixed μ, if x_r∈M_r(μ) stayed a definite distance from M_0(μ), a subsequence x_r→x would satisfy d(x,M_0(μ))≥δ. Normalized small-ball masses μ(B(y,r))/(α_d r^d) converge to the density φ(y) at continuity points. Since x_r maximizes the ball mass, the limit would force φ(y)≤φ(x) for all y, making x another density maximum, contradiction. For varying μ', first rotate supports so they lie in a common d-sphere and converge in the Hausdorff sense. A density estimate gives max φ≤2^{n+1}/vol_d(S), preventing blow-up when support volume stays nonzero. A compactness lemma for bounded concave functions on converging convex bodies gives subsequential uniform convergence of densities on compact subsets of the relative interior and an upper-semicontinuity bound at the boundary. The same maximizing-ball contradiction then proves the stronger convergence. Uniform convergence on compact families follows by compactness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5 (waist lower bound from q-dimensional adapted mass)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume f:S^n→R^q is a generic smooth map. Let r_i→0 and let Π_i∈CP_{≤q}∩F_{r_i} be convex partitions of dimension at most q that are r_i-adapted to f. Then for every ε>0, max_{z∈R^q} vol(f^{-1}(z)+ε)/vol(S^n) ≥ [vol(S^{n-q}+ε)/vol(S^n)] · limsup_{i→∞} Π_i(MC_q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each i, r_i-adaptedness gives z_i∈R^q such that for every μ∈support(Π_i) there is x_{i,μ}∈conv(M_{r_i}(μ)) with f(x_{i,μ})=z_i. On any compact K⊂MC_q, Lemmas 5.12-5.16 imply that x_{i,μ} is uniformly close to M_0(μ), and Lemma 5.6 gives μ(B(x_{i,μ},ε)) close to μ(B(M_0(μ),ε)). Since x_{i,μ} lies in the fiber f^{-1}(z_i), B(x_{i,μ},ε)⊂f^{-1}(z_i)+ε. The disintegration formula applies to f^{-1}(z_i)+ε for generic smooth f, because its boundary intersects q-dimensional convex sets in zero q-volume. Hence vol(f^{-1}(z_i)+ε)/vol(S^n) ≥ ∫_K μ(B(x_{i,μ},ε)) dΠ_i(μ). Lemma 4.8 supplies μ(B(M_0(μ),ε))≥vol(S^{n-q}+ε)/vol(S^n) for μ∈MC_q. Letting i→∞ and then taking the supremum over compact K⊂MC_q yields the displayed inequality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.17 (Prékopa-Leindler concavity of ball masses)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let S⊂R^n be open and convex. Let φ:S→[0,∞) be m-concave, meaning φ^{1/m} is concave, and set μ=φ dvol_n. Then for each fixed r, the function x↦μ(B(x,r)∩S) is (m+n)-concave on S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the generalized Prékopa-Leindler inequality quoted from Bobkov-Ledoux. For α=1/m and β=α/(1+α n)=1/(m+n), if w(θx+(1-θ)y)≥M_α^{(θ)}(u(x),v(y)) pointwise, then ∫w≥M_β^{(θ)}(∫u,∫v). Apply this with u=1_{B(x,r)}φ, v=1_{B(y,r)}φ, and w=1_{B(θx+(1-θ)y,r)}φ. Convexity of balls under Minkowski combination and m-concavity of φ give the pointwise hypothesis. The conclusion is exactly (m+n)-concavity of x↦μ(B(x,r)∩S).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.18 (weak concavity of spherical ball-mass functions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists c=c(n)>0 such that for every convexely derived measure μ on S^n, every sufficiently small r>0, and every K⊂support(μ), if v_{μ,r}(x)=μ(B(x,r)), then min_{x∈conv(K)} v_{μ,r/c}(x) ≥ c · min_{x∈K} v_{μ,r}(x), after adjusting c only as a function of n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to prove the estimate along geodesic segments. In a small spherical chart, radially project to the tangent Euclidean space. If μ has k-dimensional support and density φ on S^n, extend φ homogeneously to the cone and restrict to the tangent space; the resulting Euclidean density is (n-k)-concave. Lemma 5.17 then gives concavity, with exponent depending only on n, of Euclidean ball masses. Hence at any point in the middle third of a segment, the ball mass at comparable radius is bounded below by a dimensional constant times the larger endpoint mass, and in particular by the smaller endpoint mass. The radial projection distorts distances and densities only by dimensional constants on the chosen chart. Covering a long spherical segment by boundedly many such charts gives the global weak concavity estimate with a constant depending only on n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6 (low-dimensional pieces force large small-radius fiber tubes)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There exists c=c(n)>0 such that, for sufficiently small r>0, if f:S^n→R^q and Π∈F_r∩CP_{≤q}, then max_{z∈R^q} vol(f^{-1}(z)+r/c) ≥ c · Σ_{ℓ=0}^q vol(S^{n-ℓ}+c r) Π(MC_ℓ), with constants adjusted only as functions of n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose z∈R^q witnessing r-adaptedness of Π. For each μ in support(Π), with ℓ=dim support(μ), there is x∈conv(M_r(μ)) such that f(x)=z. Therefore B(x,r/c) lies inside f^{-1}(z)+r/c. Weak concavity gives μ(B(x,r/c))≥c min_{y∈M_r(μ)} μ(B(y,r)). Since every y∈M_r(μ) maximizes the r-ball mass, this is at least c μ(B(M_0(μ),r)), where M_0(μ) is the density maximum. Lemma 4.8 compares this last quantity to the normalized volume of an r-neighborhood of an equatorial S^{n-ℓ}; after absorbing radius changes into c, this gives a lower bound proportional to vol(S^{n-ℓ}+c r)/vol(S^n). Integrating over Π with the disintegration formula yields the stated inequality.

proof_techniques.name: Binary-tree equivariant partition spaces
proof_techniques.description: The paper encodes iterative hyperplane partitions of S^n by a product of spheres indexed by internal nodes of a rooted binary tree. The automorphism group of the tree acts freely on this parameter space, and volume/image-balance conditions become the zero set of an equivariant map. Passing to the quotient gives a vector bundle section; nonvanishing of the top Stiefel-Whitney class forces a zero, which is a partition with equal volumes and common f-image of centers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A rectangle proof might try to build sweepouts or decompositions of a doubled rectangle using a similar equivariant balancing argument, forcing many pieces or slices to interact with a common fiber of a map derived from f. The key transferable feature is converting simultaneous volume-balance and image-coincidence requirements into a topological obstruction.

proof_techniques.name: Pancake refinement by restricted cutting directions
proof_techniques.description: After obtaining balanced convex partitions, the paper restricts the cutting hyperplanes at each stage to direction spheres associated with a finite set of lower-dimensional planes. Repeating enough cuts prevents any final piece from containing a sizable ball in a (q+1)-dimensional section. By compactness of convex sets, this forces each piece to lie near a q-dimensional convex set, i.e. to be a q-pancake.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem concerns rectangles with side-length inequalities; the paper explicitly notes that Euclidean rectangles are model pancakes when some side lengths are very small. A proof may use analogous decompositions of a 4-rectangle into pieces that are effectively 2-dimensional or 3-dimensional, then combine this with 2-dilation control.

proof_techniques.name: Convexely derived measure localization
proof_techniques.description: Finite convex partitions are compactified by replacing each piece with its normalized volume measure and passing to vague limits. Limits are convexely derived measures supported on lower-dimensional convex sets. This turns a limiting partition into a probability measure on the space of such localized convex measures, allowing global volume to be recovered by disintegration.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a rectangle argument produces increasingly fine partitions or sweepouts, this compactness framework suggests how to pass to a limiting family of lower-dimensional pieces without losing volume accounting. It may be useful after doubling boxes to avoid boundary effects.

proof_techniques.name: Conical lift from spherical convexity to Euclidean convexity
proof_techniques.description: To analyze densities of convexely derived measures on S^n, the paper cones convex sets to R^{n+1}. Euclidean convex-geometric facts give concavity of the limiting density on the cone, and homogeneity of the cone measure translates this into sin-concavity on spherical geodesics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For rectangles, the Euclidean side of this argument is closer to the native geometry: one can try to use Brunn-Minkowski or log/α-concavity of slice densities directly on convex subsets of boxes.

proof_techniques.name: One-dimensional comparison with the cos^m model
proof_techniques.description: The paper reduces sharp ball-mass estimates to a one-dimensional statement: a nonnegative sin^m-concave density with maximum at an endpoint is bounded below near the maximum and above away from it by a multiple of cos^m(t). Integrating this comparison with the spherical polar Jacobian sin^{d-1}(t) yields the equatorial tube-volume ratio.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A rectangular analogue might replace sin/cos by affine or log-concave one-dimensional models. This is a promising template for proving lower bounds on the amount of a convex piece lying near a preferred center or slice.

proof_techniques.name: Disintegration over infinite convex partitions
proof_techniques.description: The paper represents a limiting convex partition Π as a probability measure on convexely derived measures and proves vol(A)/vol(S^n)=∫μ(A)dΠ(μ) for sets A with sufficiently regular boundary. This allows local estimates on each convex piece to be integrated into a global lower bound for the volume of a fiber neighborhood.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a degree-one rectangle map, one often needs to integrate local filling or slicing estimates over a sweepout. This disintegration identity is a clean model for justifying such integration after passing to limiting partitions.

proof_techniques.name: Maximal ball-mass centers and semicontinuity
proof_techniques.description: Instead of relying only on arbitrary centers of convex pieces, the paper defines M_r(μ) as the set where μ(B(x,r)) is maximal. These centers behave upper-semicontinuously under vague convergence, and as r→0 they converge to the unique density maximum M_0(μ) for fixed-dimensional convexely derived measures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When a common fiber intersects only a controlled region of each piece rather than a precise center, maximal ball-mass centers give a robust substitute. This may help in rectangular sweepout arguments where exact centers are unstable under limiting or smoothing operations.

proof_techniques.name: Weak concavity of ball-mass functions via generalized Prékopa-Leindler
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that for a measure with concave density on a convex Euclidean set, the function assigning to a point the measure of a fixed-radius ball is itself concave in a weaker exponent. Transferred to the sphere by charts, this gives a weak concavity estimate: ball mass at points in the convex hull of good centers is bounded below by a dimensional constant times the endpoint mass at a comparable radius.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This can bypass an obstacle where topology only forces a fiber to meet the convex hull of favorable points, not the points themselves. In a rectangle proof, an analogous estimate could convert intersection with a convex hull into a quantitative lower bound on nearby volume or area.

proof_techniques.name: Low-dimensional mass elimination by tube-volume growth
proof_techniques.description: For smooth generic f:S^n→R^q, small tubes around fibers have volume O(r^q). Proposition 6 says any positive mass of ℓ-dimensional partition pieces with ℓ<q would force a lower bound of order r^ℓ, too large as r→0. Therefore the limiting adapted partition must put asymptotically all its mass on q-dimensional pieces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem has a 2-dilation hypothesis and degree-one topology; a similar contradiction between expected tube or slice growth and forced lower bounds from lower-dimensional pieces might yield side-length inequalities.

proof_techniques.name: Approximation by smooth generic maps and tubular semicontinuity
proof_techniques.description: After proving the waist estimate for smooth generic maps, the paper approximates a continuous map uniformly by such maps. If g_j→f and z_j is a good value for g_j, then g_j^{-1}(z_j) lies in a small preimage neighborhood for f. Passing to a subsequential limit z and using Fatou's lemma gives the desired lower bound for f^{-1}(z)+ε.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original map is only piecewise smooth. This approximation-and-semicontinuity pattern is useful for reducing to generic transversality assumptions while preserving dilation or degree estimates up to controlled error.

proof_techniques.name: Bishop-Gromov comparison inside convex subsets
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the monotonicity of vol(S∩B(x,r))/vol(B(x,r)) for convex S⊂S^n to show that every normalized convex piece contains at least the ambient fraction of any ball centered in it. This estimate survives passage to convexely derived limits.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For rectangular or doubled rectangular domains, analogous relative volume lower bounds for convex pieces could supply crude but robust estimates when sharp localization is unnecessary.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly remarks that in Euclidean space the typical examples of pancakes are rectangles: an n-dimensional rectangle is a k-pancake when the sizes of n-k side directions are very small. It cites Gromov 2003 for the original waist theorem, Gromov-Milman 1987 and Alesker for localization and convexity ideas, and Bobkov-Ledoux for the generalized Prékopa-Leindler inequality. The introduction also mentions Gromov's related metric measurements such as k-widths and k-volumes of maps, which are conceptually close to dilation and sweepout estimates.
why_relevant: This paper provides a detailed proof of Gromov's waist theorem. Its rigorous treatment of waist/sweepout estimates can be useful if the rectangle problem is approached by embedding or doubling rectangles and applying quantitative waist arguments.
